title: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine
authors: Susan M. Domchek, J. Larry Jameson

items.subtopic: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice
items.title: Role of Genetic Testing in Disease Risk Assessment
items.content: Genetic testing is increasingly utilized to identify inherited abnormalities that influence disease risk. This includes chromosomal abnormalities, specific gene mutations with autosomal patterns, and SNPs associated with small relative risks. These genetic factors contribute to both classic Mendelian conditions and common adult-onset diseases like cancer.
keyPoints: Genetic testing identifies germline alterations responsible for a range of disorders.
Common adult-onset diseases influenced by genetics include asthma, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, macular degeneration, and various cancers.
There is a complex interplay between multiple genes and environmental factors affecting disease risk, onset, severity, and treatment.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice

items.subtopic: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice
items.title: Impact of Genetic Knowledge on Disease Management
items.content: Advancements in genetic knowledge are reshaping our understanding of pathophysiology and disease classification. This awareness influences clinical management, including prevention, screening, and treatment strategies for genetically influenced diseases.
keyPoints: Primary care physicians must understand the genetic basis of many diseases to guide testing and counseling.
Physicians should identify patients meeting guidelines for genetic testing and provide information on its indications, limitations, risks, and benefits.
The transition from single-gene to multigene panel testing increases complexity in test selection, interpretation, patient education, and decision-making.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice

items.title: Understanding Penetrance in Autosomal Dominant Conditions
items.content: Autosomal dominant conditions like hereditary breast and ovarian cancer (due to BRCA1/2) and Lynch syndrome have high penetrance, meaning a significant lifetime risk of cancer. However, penetrance is incomplete, not reaching 100%. In contrast, conditions like CHEK2-associated breast cancer show lower penetrance, with a moderate lifetime risk of 20-30%.
keyPoints: Hereditary breast and ovarian cancer (BRCA1/2) have high penetrance but are less than 100%.
Lynch syndrome also has high penetrance due to mismatch repair gene variants.
CHEK2-associated breast cancer shows moderate lifetime risk of 20-30%, compared to 50-70% for BRCA1/2.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · INHERITANCE PATTERNS

items.title: Evaluating Multifactorial and Polygenic Risks in Adult-Onset Disorders
items.content: Many adult-onset disorders are multifactorial, with risk conferred by multiple genetic loci (polygenic) each having small effects. A polygenic risk score (PRS) can be calculated from these loci to assess disease risk. While PRS and SNP panels are available for assessing risks like cardiovascular disease, their optimal clinical use remains uncertain.
keyPoints: Multifactorial adult-onset disorders involve multiple genetic loci with small effects.
Polygenic risk scores (PRS) combine these loci to assess disease risk.
SNP panels provide PRS but their optimal clinical application is still under study.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · INHERITANCE PATTERNS

items.title: Interpreting Family History to Assess Genetic Risk
items.content: Family history, recorded as pedigrees, helps assess genetic risk by considering the number of affected relatives and their relation degree. Expanded pedigrees are necessary when inherited disease is suggested.
keyPoints: A pedigree includes health-related data on first- and second-degree relatives.
Risk assessment varies with family size, number of unaffected relatives, diagnosis types, and age of onset.
Example: A woman with 3 female first-degree relatives (1 affected) has higher risk than one with 10 (8 unaffected).
Consider factors like adoption, limited family structure, young disease onset, unusual diseases, and multiple related diseases.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Identifying Genetic Disorders through Physical Examination Clues
items.content: Physical examination findings can indicate specific inherited disorders. For instance, xanthomas suggest familial hypercholesterolemia and trichilemmomas in a woman with breast cancer may indicate Cowden syndrome.
keyPoints: Xanthomas at a young age prompt consideration of familial hypercholesterolemia.
Trichilemmomas in a woman with breast cancer raise concern for Cowden syndrome, associated with PTEN pathogenic variants.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Considerations and Challenges in Family History Analysis
items.content: Family history recall can be inaccurate, especially if remote. Confirming reported illnesses is crucial before making management decisions. Disease penetrance is often incomplete, complicating pedigree analysis.
keyPoints: Recall of family history is often inaccurate; use forms and electronic health records to improve accuracy.
Confirm illnesses through interviews or medical record reviews before major decisions.
Incomplete disease penetrance means not all carriers show symptoms, affecting Mendelian pattern clarity.
Genes like BRCA2 can lead to variable disease expression across families.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 1)

items.subtopic: FAMILY HISTORY (part 2) | GENETIC TESTING FOR ADULT-ONSET DISORDERS
items.title: Optimal Strategy for BRCA1/BRCA2 Testing in Families with Cancer History
items.content: In families with a history of breast and ovarian cancer, testing the most affected relative first can be cost-effective. This approach helps identify pathogenic variants that may have been inherited by other family members.
keyPoints: Testing the most affected relative (e.g., 42-year-old cousin) for BRCA1/BRCA2 is informative and cost-effective.
If a pathogenic variant is found, it can be tested in other relatives.
A positive test in the father indicates a 50:50 chance of transmission to offspring.
A negative result in the father rules out inheritance of that specific variant.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 2) | GENETIC TESTING FOR ADULT-ONSET DISORDERS

items.title: Importance of Accurate Clinical Diagnosis Before Genetic Testing
items.content: Before genetic testing, ensure a correct clinical diagnosis based on family history, physical findings, pathology, or biochemical tests to define the phenotype and guide gene selection.
keyPoints: Clinical assessment defines the phenotype before genetic testing.
Testing should target probable genes based on phenotype to avoid unnecessary tests.
Locus heterogeneity means different genes can cause similar disorders.
Expanded testing techniques allow sequencing of many genes or whole exome simultaneously.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 2) | GENETIC TESTING FOR ADULT-ONSET DISORDERS

items.title: Regulation and Quality Assurance in Genetic Testing
items.content: Genetic testing laboratories in the United States are required to be CLIA approved, ensuring they meet quality and proficiency standards. This regulation is crucial for maintaining the reliability of genetic test results.
keyPoints: CLIA approval ensures quality and proficiency standards in genetic testing laboratories.
www.genetests.org is a useful information source for various genetic tests.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · METHODOLOGIC APPROACHES TO GENETIC TESTING

items.title: Techniques and Applications of Genetic Testing
items.content: Genetic testing primarily involves DNA sequence analysis, but can also include RNA or protein studies. Techniques like PCR enable rapid amplification and analysis from various tissue sources, while massively parallel sequencing allows examination of many genes simultaneously.
keyPoints: DNA sequence analysis is the primary method for detecting pathogenic variants.
PCR enables genetic testing on minimal DNA amounts from diverse tissues.
Massively parallel sequencing examines multiple genes at once, altering approaches to hereditary disorder testing.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · METHODOLOGIC APPROACHES TO GENETIC TESTING

items.title: Challenges and Evolution of Genetic Testing Strategies
items.content: The advent of multiplex panels and whole exome sequencing (WEX) has expanded genetic testing capabilities, though challenges include variants of uncertain significance (VUS) and unexpected results. These advancements have shifted clinical practices towards more comprehensive panel-based testing.
keyPoints: Multiplex panels test multiple genes associated with specific conditions.
Whole exome sequencing is used for unexplained syndromes and may become more common as costs decrease.
Challenges include VUS identification and unexpected clinically relevant results.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · METHODOLOGIC APPROACHES TO GENETIC TESTING

items.title: Understanding the Differences Between Clinician-Directed and Direct-to-Consumer Genetic Testing
items.content: Clinician-directed genetic testing is based on personal or family medical history, with results disclosed by a clinician who formulates a management plan. In contrast, direct-to-consumer (DTC) genetic testing allows individuals to request tests from DTC companies independently, receiving results directly without clinical oversight.
keyPoints: Clinician-directed testing is based on personal or family medical history.
DTC testing involves elective requests by individuals to DTC companies.
Results of DTC testing are communicated directly to the consumer.

Quality standards vary: Clinical labs have CLIA certification and CAP accreditation; some DTC labs have FDA marketing authorization but often include disclaimers against using results for medical management.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · DIRECT-TO-CONSUMER GENETIC TESTING

items.title: Clinical Utility and Quality Standards in Direct-to-Consumer Genetic Testing
items.content: DTC genetic testing primarily examines SNPs associated with disease risks, but does not provide definitive risk assessments. Clinical genetic tests are more thorough, focusing on specific genes related to diseases. Quality standards differ significantly between DTC labs and clinical laboratories.
keyPoints: DTC testing often involves SNP analysis for disease risk or carrier status.
SNP analysis in DTC testing does not provide definitive risk assessments.
Clinical genetic tests thoroughly interrogate specific genes associated with diseases.
Quality standards: Clinical labs have CLIA certification and CAP accreditation; some DTC labs have FDA marketing authorization but often include disclaimers.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · DIRECT-TO-CONSUMER GENETIC TESTING

items.title: Why Genetic Testing May Yield Uninformative Results
items.content: Genetic tests can sometimes yield uninformative results due to their design limitations, which may only detect common pathogenic variants. Additionally, the evolution of genetic testing over time means that certain comprehensive tests were not available until recently, such as large genomic rearrangement testing for BRCA1 and BRCA2 genes in 2006.
keyPoints: Genetic tests often target common pathogenic variants, potentially missing rare or novel ones.
Comprehensive genomic rearrangement testing for BRCA1/BRCA2 was not available until 2006.
A negative result may be uninformative if the test did not cover all possible pathogenic variants.
Medical management should rely on personal and family history when genetic results are uninformative.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · LIMITATIONS OF GENETIC TESTING

items.title: How Variants of Uncertain Significance (VUS) Complicate Genetic Testing
items.content: The identification of a VUS in genetic testing presents challenges because the impact on protein function is unknown. As more genes are sequenced, the likelihood of encountering one or more VUSs increases, complicating medical management decisions.
keyPoints: A VUS indicates an alteration with unknown effects on protein function.
Most VUSs will eventually be reclassified as benign, but some may be pathogenic.
The prevalence of VUS findings rises with increased gene sequencing (e.g., multiplex panels or WES).
Medical management in the presence of a VUS should be based on personal and family history until further classification.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · LIMITATIONS OF GENETIC TESTING

items.title: Presymptomatic vs. Predisposition Testing in Genetic Disorders
items.content: Presymptomatic testing is used for diseases with a near 100% likelihood of developing if a specific genetic alteration is present, such as Huntington's disease. In contrast, predisposition testing applies to conditions where the risk is less than 100%, like hereditary colon cancer.
keyPoints: Presymptomatic testing: near 100% likelihood of disease development
Predisposition testing: <100% risk for disease
Examples: Huntington's disease (presymptomatic), hereditary colon cancer (predisposition)
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · TESTING STRATEGIES

items.title: Initiating Genetic Testing in Families with Known Pathogenic Variants
items.content: Testing should begin with a family member affected by the disease to identify a pathogenic variant. This allows for targeted testing of other at-risk members and provides reassurance to those who test negative.
keyPoints: Start testing with an affected family member
Identify pathogenic variants to guide further testing
Negative results in absence of additional risks mean general population risk
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · TESTING STRATEGIES

items.title: Considerations for Genetic Testing in Children for Adult-Onset Disorders
items.content: Genetic testing for adult-onset disorders is generally not recommended for children due to lack of early interventions and potential psychosocial risks. Exceptions include conditions like MEN 2 and FAP, where early management can prevent severe outcomes.
keyPoints: Avoid genetic testing for adult-onset disorders in children
Exceptions: MEN 2 (prophylactic thyroidectomy), FAP (colonoscopy screening from ages 10-15)
Psychosocial risks and lack of childhood interventions are key concerns
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · TESTING STRATEGIES

items.subtopic: INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING
items.title: Understanding Informed Consent and Genetic Testing Implications
items.content: Informed consent in genetic testing involves educating individuals about the risks, benefits, limitations of the tests, and potential long-term implications. This includes understanding medical, psychological, and social consequences, as well as awareness of protections under GINA against job and health insurance discrimination.
keyPoints: Genetic testing can predict future risk for asymptomatic individuals.
GINA provides some protection but not for disability, long-term care, or life insurance.
Alternatives to genetic testing include delaying the test or DNA banking.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING

items.subtopic: INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING
items.title: Posttest Interventions and Continued Risk Awareness
items.content: After genetic testing, interventions depend on the disorder. For BRCA1/2 pathogenic variants, risk-reducing surgeries and intensive screenings are recommended. Patients with negative test results for a family-associated gene should still follow general population screening guidelines.
keyPoints: BRCA1/2 variant carriers may undergo salpingo-oophorectomy and enhanced breast cancer screening including MRI.
Chemoprevention options include tamoxifen, raloxifene, or exemestane.
Negative test results do not eliminate risk for common diseases; general population screenings are still necessary.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING

items.subtopic: POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS
items.title: Rationale for Population Screening: Identifying Asymptomatic Carriers
items.content: Population screening for germline disease-associated variants is advocated due to the identification of pathogenic variants in individuals without a personal or family history that meets current testing guidelines. This approach has been exemplified by studies from large biobanks and implemented in Israel for Ashkenazi Jewish descent.
keyPoints: Many individuals with pathogenic variants lack personal/family history meeting current genetic testing guidelines.
In Israel, insurance covers BRCA1/2 founder mutations screening for all Ashkenazi Jews (found in 2.5%).
Challenges include variable penetrance and consensus on gene inclusion.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS

items.subtopic: POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS
items.title: Challenges and Considerations in Population Screening Implementation
items.content: Implementing population screening for germline variants involves addressing challenges such as variable penetrance, consensus on gene inclusion, management of Variants of Uncertain Significance (VUS), and practical issues like insurance coverage and counseling limitations under GINA.
keyPoints: Variable penetrance may lower disease risk in those without classic family history.
Consensus needed on which genes to include in screening panels.
Practical challenges include insurance, counseling limitations, and potential socioeconomic/racial disparities.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE
items.title: Management of Hereditary Hemochromatosis with Early Intervention
items.content: Hereditary hemochromatosis, primarily caused by pathogenic variants in the HFE gene, leads to iron overload and associated complications such as liver disease and diabetes. Early diagnosis allows for effective management through therapeutic phlebotomy.
keyPoints: Pathogenic variants in HFE cause hereditary hemochromatosis.
Manifestations include iron overload, liver disease, skin pigmentation, diabetes mellitus, arthropathy, impotence in males, and cardiac issues.
Early diagnosis enables management with therapeutic phlebotomy.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE
items.title: Preventative Strategies for Familial Cardiac Conditions
items.content: Familial long QT syndrome and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy can be managed with early detection and specific interventions. Long QT syndrome involves prophylactic antiarrhythmic therapy, while hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is treated with beta blockers and lifestyle modifications.
keyPoints: Familial long QT syndrome requires early electrocardiographic testing and prophylactic antiarrhythmic therapy or defibrillators.
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy management includes ultrasound screening, beta blockers, and avoiding strenuous exercise.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE
items.title: Molecularly Directed Therapies for Genetic Diseases
items.content: Advancements in molecular therapies offer targeted treatments for genetic diseases. Examples include transthyretin stabilizers for cardiac amyloidosis and PARP inhibitors for BRCA1/2-associated cancers.
keyPoints: Transthyretin stabilizers are used for TTR-associated cardiac amyloidosis.
PARP inhibitors treat BRCA1/2 and PALB2-associated cancers.
Gene therapy is emerging for conditions like spinal muscular atrophy and sickle cell disease.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE

items.subtopic: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice
items.question: Which type of genetic alteration is associated with small relative risks for disease?
options: Chromosomal abnormalities
Specific gene pathogenic variants
Single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs)
Autosomal recessive patterns
items.explanation: Single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) are associated with small relative risks for disease.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice

items.subtopic: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice
items.question: What is the role of primary care physicians in genetic testing and management?
options: They perform all genetic tests themselves.
They only refer patients to genetic specialists.
They help navigate testing and treatment options, understanding genetic basis for diseases.
They do not need to understand genetics as it is outside their scope.
items.explanation: Primary care physicians help patients navigate testing and treatment options and must understand the genetic basis for many diseases.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice

items.subtopic: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice
items.question: What has increased the complexity of test selection and interpretation in clinical genetics?
options: Reduction in available genetic tests.
Shift from multigene panel testing to single-gene testing.
Introduction of whole exome and genome sequencing techniques.
Decrease in genetic knowledge.
items.explanation: The introduction of whole exome and genome sequencing has increased the complexity of test selection and interpretation.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice

items.question: Which gene mutation is associated with a high penetrance of breast and ovarian cancer?
options: BRCA1 and BRCA2 pathogenic variants
CHEK2 pathogenic variants
MUTYH biallelic pathogenic variants
MLH1 germline pathogenic variants
items.explanation: Germline BRCA1 and BRCA2 pathogenic variants are associated with a high penetrance of breast and ovarian cancer.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · INHERITANCE PATTERNS

items.question: What is the mode of inheritance for Lynch syndrome?
options: Autosomal recessive
X-linked dominant
Autosomal dominant
Mitochondrial
items.explanation: Lynch syndrome follows an autosomal dominant pattern of inheritance.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · INHERITANCE PATTERNS

items.question: Which condition is transmitted in an autosomal recessive fashion and involves biallelic pathogenic variants?
options: Hemochromatosis
Lynch syndrome
Hereditary breast cancer
CHEK2-associated breast cancer
items.explanation: Hemochromatosis is transmitted in an autosomal recessive fashion and involves biallelic pathogenic variants.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · INHERITANCE PATTERNS

items.question: Which factor increases the likelihood of a specific Mendelian disorder risk assessment when evaluating a woman with two first-degree relatives who have breast cancer?
options: Having a total of 10 female first-degree relatives, with 8 unaffected
Having a total of 3 female first-degree relatives, with only 1 unaffected
Having no male relatives in the family history
Having multiple early deaths unrelated to the target disease
items.explanation: A woman with two first-degree relatives with breast cancer is at greater risk for a specific Mendelian disorder if she has a total of 3 female first-degree relatives (with only 1 unaffected).
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 1)

items.question: What does the presence of trichilemmomas in a woman with breast cancer suggest?
options: A high likelihood of familial hypercholesterolemia
An increased risk for Cowden syndrome associated with PTEN pathogenic variants
A nonhereditary risk factor for breast cancer
A phenocopy related to breast cancer
items.explanation: The presence of trichilemmomas in a woman with breast cancer raises concern for Cowden syndrome, associated with PTEN pathogenic variants.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 1)

items.question: What is suggested by the absence of nonhereditary risk factors typically associated with a disease?
options: A decreased likelihood of a genetic disorder
An increased concern about a genetic association
No impact on the assessment of genetic disorders
A confirmation of phenocopies in the family history
items.explanation: The absence of nonhereditary risk factors typically associated with a disease raises concern about a genetic association.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 1)

items.question: What complicates the pedigree analysis in families with inherited disorders?
options: Complete penetrance of genetic disorders
Clustering of relatives with unrelated conditions
Incomplete disease penetrance and variable expression
Accurate recall of family history by all members
items.explanation: Disease penetrance is incomplete for most genetic disorders, and genes often exhibit variable disease expression.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 1)

items.subtopic: FAMILY HISTORY (part 2) | GENETIC TESTING FOR ADULT-ONSET DISORDERS
items.question: In a family with a history of breast and ovarian cancer, what is the most informative and cost-effective approach to DNA analysis?
options: Test the proband directly for BRCA1 or BRCA2 pathogenic variants.
Test the 42-year-old living cousin who has been affected by cancer.
Perform whole exome sequencing on all family members.
Conduct genetic testing only after confirming a diagnosis in every member.
items.explanation: Testing the cancer-affected cousin can identify if a pathogenic variant is present, allowing for targeted testing of other relatives.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 2) | GENETIC TESTING FOR ADULT-ONSET DISORDERS

items.subtopic: FAMILY HISTORY (part 2) | GENETIC TESTING FOR ADULT-ONSET DISORDERS
items.question: What should be confirmed before initiating genetic testing for adult-onset disorders?
options: A detailed family history.
The correct clinical diagnosis based on various assessments.
The availability of advanced genetic testing techniques.
The proband's desire to undergo genetic testing.
items.explanation: Ensuring the correct clinical diagnosis is essential before proceeding with genetic testing.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 2) | GENETIC TESTING FOR ADULT-ONSET DISORDERS

items.subtopic: FAMILY HISTORY (part 2) | GENETIC TESTING FOR ADULT-ONSET DISORDERS
items.question: What does locus heterogeneity refer to in the context of genetic disorders?
options: The presence of multiple pathogenic variants within a single gene.
Pathogenic variants in different genes causing similar phenotypes.
Variability in disease expression among family members.
The inheritance pattern of genetic disorders.
items.explanation: Locus heterogeneity refers to the phenomenon where pathogenic variants in different genes can lead to similar clinical conditions.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 2) | GENETIC TESTING FOR ADULT-ONSET DISORDERS

items.question: Which technology allows for the rapid amplification and analysis of a gene of interest in genetic testing?
options: Polymerase chain reaction (PCR)
Fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH)
Southern blotting
Cytogenetics
items.explanation: PCR allows rapid amplification and analysis of the gene of interest.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · METHODOLOGIC APPROACHES TO GENETIC TESTING

items.question: What is a major challenge associated with multiplex panels for inherited susceptibility?
options: They are too expensive
They only test a limited number of genes
They often include genes with no validated association with the disease
They cannot detect large genomic alterations
items.explanation: Multiplex panels often contain genes with no validated association or modest association for which clinical utility is uncertain.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · METHODOLOGIC APPROACHES TO GENETIC TESTING

items.question: Which method involves sequencing millions of small fragments of DNA in parallel?
options: Sanger sequencing
Fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH)
Massively parallel sequencing
Southern blotting
items.explanation: Massively parallel sequencing sequences millions of small DNA fragments in parallel.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · METHODOLOGIC APPROACHES TO GENETIC TESTING

items.question: Which type of genetic testing is now commercially available and used for individuals with syndromes unexplained by traditional methods?
options: Whole genome sequencing
Fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH)
Polymerase chain reaction (PCR)
Southern blotting
items.explanation: Whole exome sequencing is used for individuals with unexplained syndromes.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · METHODOLOGIC APPROACHES TO GENETIC TESTING

items.question: Which of the following best describes a key difference between clinician-directed and consumer-directed genetic testing?
options: Clinician-directed testing is initiated by the patient, while consumer-directed testing is requested by the clinician.
Consumer-directed testing results are communicated directly to the consumer without clinician involvement.
Both types of testing require FDA marketing authorization for all tests conducted.
Clinical genetic testing often examines sets of SNPs similar to DTC testing.
items.explanation: Consumer-directed testing involves individuals requesting and receiving results directly from a company, bypassing clinician involvement.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · DIRECT-TO-CONSUMER GENETIC TESTING

items.question: Which statement accurately reflects the quality standards of DTC genetic testing labs compared to clinical laboratories in the United States?
options: DTC labs are required to meet CLIA certification and CAP accreditation.
Clinical genetic testing labs do not need FDA marketing authorization.
Quality standards between DTC and clinical labs vary greatly, with clinical labs meeting stricter criteria like CLIA and CAP.
All genetic testing labs in the U.S. must adhere to the same quality standards.
items.explanation: Clinical laboratories meet strict quality standards such as CLIA certification and CAP accreditation, unlike many DTC labs.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · DIRECT-TO-CONSUMER GENETIC TESTING

items.question: What is a common limitation of SNP analysis in direct-to-consumer genetic testing?
options: SNP analysis provides definitive risk/no risk answers.
SNP analysis only examines rare mutations not associated with diseases.
SNP analysis does not provide a definitive risk/no risk answer.
SNP analysis is identical to clinical genetic testing in terms of depth and accuracy.
items.explanation: SNP analysis in DTC testing identifies common gene variants but cannot definitively determine disease risk.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · DIRECT-TO-CONSUMER GENETIC TESTING

items.question: Which of the following best describes a limitation of genetic testing related to variant detection?
options: Genetic tests are designed to detect only the most common pathogenic variants.
All possible pathogenic variants in genes can be detected with current technology.
Genetic tests have no limitations regarding the types of variants they can detect.
Genetic testing is always comprehensive and includes all known cancer-associated genes.
items.explanation: Genetic tests are sometimes designed to detect only the most common pathogenic variants, limiting their ability to identify less common or novel variants.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · LIMITATIONS OF GENETIC TESTING

items.question: What is a potential consequence of receiving an uninformative negative result from genetic testing?
options: The individual can be certain they do not carry any pathogenic variant.
Medical management decisions should still consider personal and family history.
No further medical action or screening is necessary.
The test guarantees that no other cancer-associated genes are involved.
items.explanation: An uninformative negative result means the genetic testing did not detect a variant, so decisions should still be based on personal and family history.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · LIMITATIONS OF GENETIC TESTING

items.question: What is a challenge associated with finding a VUS in genetic testing?
options: VUS findings always lead to immediate changes in medical management.
The effect of the variant on protein function is known and well-documented.
Finding a VUS complicates decisions regarding ongoing screening and care.
All VUSs are eventually classified as pathogenic.
items.explanation: A VUS indicates an unknown effect on protein function, making it challenging to determine appropriate medical management until further reclassification occurs.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · LIMITATIONS OF GENETIC TESTING

items.question: Which type of genetic testing predicts a risk for disease that is less than 100%?
options: Presymptomatic testing
Predisposition testing
Diagnostic testing
Carrier testing
items.explanation: Predisposition testing predicts a risk for disease that is <100%, unlike presymptomatic testing which has near certainty.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · TESTING STRATEGIES

items.question: For which condition is presymptomatic genetic testing available?
options: Hereditary colon cancer
Huntington's disease
Alzheimer's disease
Familial adenomatous polyposis
items.explanation: Presymptomatic testing is available for Huntington's disease, where a specific genetic alteration indicates near certainty of developing the disease.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · TESTING STRATEGIES

items.question: What should be considered before initiating genetic testing in individuals?
options: The cost of the test
Availability of treatment options
Pretest education and patient's coping ability
Insurance coverage
items.explanation: Pretest education is important, as well as assessing the patient's ability to understand and cope with test results.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · TESTING STRATEGIES

items.question: Why should genetic testing for adult-onset disorders generally not be offered to children?
options: Children cannot provide consent
There are no interventions in childhood to prevent the disease
Testing is too expensive for minors
Genetic information can cause anxiety in parents
items.explanation: Many adult-onset disorders have no known interventions in childhood, and testing poses significant psychosocial risks.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · TESTING STRATEGIES

items.subtopic: INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a protection provided by the Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act (GINA)?
options: Job discrimination
Health insurance discrimination
Disability coverage
Life insurance coverage
items.explanation: GINA does not address disability, long-term care, and life insurance coverage.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING

items.subtopic: INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING
items.question: What is one of the potential posttest interventions for individuals with deleterious pathogenic variants in BRCA1 or BRCA2?
options: Risk avoidance
Intensive breast cancer screening
Physical therapy
DNA banking
items.explanation: Patients are offered intensive breast cancer screening with MRI and risk-reducing mastectomy.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING

items.subtopic: INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING
items.question: What should individuals who test negative for a pathogenic variant in a disease-associated gene identified in an affected family member be reminded of?
options: They are completely free from the risk of that disease.
They may still require standard screening recommended for the general population.
They should undergo immediate genetic counseling again.
They can ignore all future medical advice related to that condition.
items.explanation: Such individuals may still be at risk and need standard screening as recommended for the general population.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING

items.subtopic: POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS
items.question: Which population has insurance coverage for screening of three common BRCA1 and BRCA2 founder mutations?
options: All individuals in the United States
Individuals with a family history of breast cancer
All individuals of Ashkenazi Jewish descent in Israel
Only those who meet current guidelines for genetic testing
items.explanation: In Israel, insurance covers all individuals of Ashkenazi Jewish descent for the three common BRCA1 and BRCA2 founder mutations.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS

items.subtopic: POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS
items.question: What is one challenge in considering population screening for germline disease-associated variants?
options: High costs of genetic testing
Lack of evidence for clinical utility
Variable penetrance impacted by family history
Universal consensus on genes to include
items.explanation: One challenge is variable penetrance, which may be significantly impacted by family history.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS

items.subtopic: POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS
items.question: What percentage of individuals of Ashkenazi Jewish descent carry the common BRCA1 and BRCA2 founder mutations?
items.explanation: The three common BRCA1 and BRCA2 founder mutations are found in 2.5% of individuals of Ashkenazi Jewish descent.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE
items.question: Which of the following is a recommended management strategy for hereditary hemochromatosis when diagnosed early?
options: Therapeutic phlebotomy
Prophylactic surgery
Use of overdrive pacemakers
Gene therapy
items.explanation: Early management of hereditary hemochromatosis involves therapeutic phlebotomy to reduce iron overload.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE
items.question: What is a common intervention for familial hypertrophic cardiomyopathy?
options: Beta blockers or other drugs
Gene therapy
Therapeutic phlebotomy
Chemoprevention
items.explanation: Familial hypertrophic cardiomyopathy can be treated with beta blockers or other drugs.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE
items.question: Which molecularly directed therapy is used for TTR-associated cardiac amyloid?
options: Poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase inhibitors
Transthyretin stabilizers
Exon skipping medications
Gene replacement therapy
items.explanation: Transthyretin stabilizers are used for TTR-associated cardiac amyloid.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE
items.question: Which genetic disorder is associated with the use of PARP inhibitors?
options: Familial hemochromatosis
Long QT syndrome
BRCA1/2-associated cancers
Marfan's syndrome
items.explanation: PARP inhibitors are used for the treatment of BRCA1/2 and PALB2-associated breast, ovarian, prostate, and pancreatic cancer.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE

items.subtopic: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice
items.statement: Germline alterations are only responsible for classic Mendelian conditions.
items.explanation: Germline alterations are also responsible for genetic susceptibility to common adult-onset diseases beyond classic Mendelian conditions.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice

items.subtopic: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice
items.statement: Primary care physicians need not be involved in the process of genetic testing and counseling.
items.explanation: Primary care physicians play a crucial role in helping patients navigate genetic testing and treatment options.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice

items.statement: Polygenic risk scores (PRS) are used to assess the risk of diseases with single-gene mutations.
items.explanation: PRS are used for assessing risk in polygenic disorders, not single-gene mutations.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · INHERITANCE PATTERNS

items.statement: A healthy lifestyle can mitigate the risk associated with elevated polygenic risk diseases.
items.explanation: Data suggest that a healthy lifestyle can help reduce the risk of diseases influenced by polygenic factors.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · INHERITANCE PATTERNS

items.statement: Recall of family history is typically accurate.
items.explanation: Recall of family history is often inaccurate, especially when the history is remote.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 1)

items.statement: Pathogenic variants in BRCA2 can predispose to multiple malignancies within the same family.
items.explanation: Pathogenic variants in the breast cancer-associated gene BRCA2 can predispose to several different malignancies, including cancers of the breast, ovary, pancreas, and prostate.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 1)

items.subtopic: FAMILY HISTORY (part 2) | GENETIC TESTING FOR ADULT-ONSET DISORDERS
items.statement: If a proband's father tests negative for the known familial BRCA1 pathogenic variant, the proband and her brother are at risk of having inherited this variant.
items.explanation: A true-negative result in the father indicates that he did not have the familial BRCA1 pathogenic variant to pass on.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 2) | GENETIC TESTING FOR ADULT-ONSET DISORDERS

items.subtopic: FAMILY HISTORY (part 2) | GENETIC TESTING FOR ADULT-ONSET DISORDERS
items.statement: Rapid developments in genetic testing techniques allow for sequencing many genes or even the whole exome at one time.
items.explanation: Technological advancements have made it feasible to conduct comprehensive genetic tests simultaneously.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 2) | GENETIC TESTING FOR ADULT-ONSET DISORDERS

items.statement: Genetic testing laboratories in the United States must be CLIA approved.
items.explanation: CLIA approval ensures quality and proficiency standards for genetic testing labs.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · METHODOLOGIC APPROACHES TO GENETIC TESTING

items.statement: Whole genome sequencing is currently the most cost-effective method of genetic analysis.
items.explanation: Although feasible, whole genome sequencing involves challenges like data interpretation and high costs.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · METHODOLOGIC APPROACHES TO GENETIC TESTING

items.statement: DTC genetic tests are always integrated into the patient's medical record.
items.explanation: Integration of DTC test results into a medical record requires action by the patient's physician or healthcare provider.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · DIRECT-TO-CONSUMER GENETIC TESTING

items.statement: Some DTC labs have FDA marketing authorization but often include disclaimers regarding their use for medical management.
items.explanation: FDA authorization does not equate to clinical utility, and results may come with disclaimers.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · DIRECT-TO-CONSUMER GENETIC TESTING

items.statement: Genetic testing can always provide a definitive answer regarding the presence of pathogenic variants in all genes.
items.explanation: Genetic tests may not detect all possible pathogenic variants, especially those that are less common or novel.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · LIMITATIONS OF GENETIC TESTING

items.statement: The discovery of a VUS means the variant's impact on disease risk is well understood.
items.explanation: A VUS indicates that the effect of the variant on protein function and disease risk is not known.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · LIMITATIONS OF GENETIC TESTING

items.statement: Genetic test results can predict with certainty whether a disease will manifest.
items.explanation: Test results cannot reveal with confidence whether, when, or how the disease will manifest itself.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · TESTING STRATEGIES

items.statement: Individuals who test negative for a familial pathogenic variant are at general population risk for that particular disease.
items.explanation: In the absence of additional risk factors, those testing negative have the same risk as the general population.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · TESTING STRATEGIES

items.subtopic: INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING
items.statement: The Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act (GINA) provides protections against discrimination in disability, long-term care, and life insurance coverage.
items.explanation: GINA does not address disability, long-term care, or life insurance coverage.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING

items.subtopic: INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING
items.statement: Patients with deleterious pathogenic variants in BRCA1 or BRCA2 are recommended to undergo risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy.
items.explanation: Such patients are strongly encouraged to undergo this procedure at an appropriate age.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING

items.subtopic: POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS
items.statement: Population screening for germline disease-associated variants can identify pathogenic variants in individuals without a personal or family history.
items.explanation: Studies have shown that many individuals with pathogenic variants do not have a personal or family history meeting current guidelines.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS

items.subtopic: POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS
items.statement: Variable penetrance is not considered a challenge in population screening for germline disease-associated variants.
items.explanation: Variable penetrance, impacted by family history, is indeed a challenge in considering population screening.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE
items.statement: Gene therapy for sickle cell disease involves increasing production of fetal hemoglobin or hemoglobin A.
items.explanation: Gene therapy in sickle cell disease can increase the production of fetal hemoglobin or hemoglobin A.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE
items.statement: Incorporating CYP2C6 and VKORC1 genotyping into clinical practice has been shown to improve patient outcomes for warfarin dosing.
items.explanation: There is no evidence that incorporating CYP2C6 and VKORC1 genotyping improves patient outcomes compared with clinical algorithms.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE

items.subtopic: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice
items.assertion: Awareness of genetic etiology can impact clinical management.
items.reason: It influences prevention, screening, and treatment options for a range of diseases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why awareness of genetic etiology impacts clinical management.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice

items.assertion: A woman with a young age of disease onset for myocardial infarction should prompt consideration of genetic factors.
items.reason: Young age of disease onset, such as a 30-year-old nonsmoking woman with a myocardial infarction, is unusual and suggests potential underlying genetic issues.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why young age of disease onset should prompt consideration of genetic factors.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 1)

items.assertion: A family history with incomplete penetrance will always show a clear Mendelian inheritance pattern.
items.reason: Incomplete penetrance means not all carriers of the disease-associated alleles manifest clinical evidence of the condition.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because incomplete penetrance often results in pedigrees that do not exhibit a clear Mendelian inheritance pattern.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · FAMILY HISTORY (part 1)

items.assertion: Massively parallel sequencing has significantly altered the approach to genetic testing for adult-onset hereditary susceptibility disorders.
items.reason: This technology allows high-throughput DNA analysis of many genes simultaneously, unlike traditional Sanger sequencing.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · METHODOLOGIC APPROACHES TO GENETIC TESTING

items.assertion: Whole exome sequencing is primarily used for individuals with well-characterized genetic conditions.
items.reason: It is mainly utilized in cases where traditional genetic testing fails to provide explanations.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; WES is used for unexplained syndromes, not well-characterized ones.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · METHODOLOGIC APPROACHES TO GENETIC TESTING

items.assertion: Preimplantation genetic testing (PGT) allows for selection of embryos without a familial pathogenic variant.
items.reason: Individuals consider PGT in their reproductive decision-making to avoid passing on the pathogenic variant.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · TESTING STRATEGIES

items.assertion: Genetic testing for adult-onset disorders should be offered to children.
items.reason: Testing during childhood violates a child's right to make an informed decision upon reaching adulthood.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true as it highlights concerns about offering such testing.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · TESTING STRATEGIES

items.subtopic: INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING
items.assertion: Individuals who test negative for a pathogenic variant in a disease-associated gene identified in an affected family member are not at risk for the disease.
items.reason: They may still be at risk and require standard screening recommended for the general population.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because they can still be at risk despite testing negative.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · INFORMED CONSENT | FOLLOW-UP CARE AFTER TESTING

items.subtopic: POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS
items.assertion: Population screening for germline disease-associated variants can lead to insurance coverage changes.
items.reason: In Israel, insurance covers BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations for all individuals of Ashkenazi Jewish descent.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · POPULATION SCREENING FOR GERMLINE DISEASE-ASSOCIATED VARIANTS

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE
items.assertion: Identification of germline abnormalities that increase cancer risk can lead to early screening and prophylactic surgery.
items.reason: This approach is applied in familial forms of melanoma, breast, ovary, and thyroid cancers.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE
items.assertion: Pharmacogenetics has been fully integrated into clinical practice for all drugs.
items.reason: It seeks to individualize drug therapy to improve outcomes and reduce toxicity.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; pharmacogenetics is not fully integrated into clinical practice for all drugs.
items.reference: The Integration of Inherited Genetics into Clinical Practice · THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS BASED ON GENETIC RISK FOR DISEASE
